items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.
It's Authoritarian personality. “Frustration level” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Autonomous to agentic. “Group to individual” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In Milgram's original study, what percentage of participants went to 300V?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
